title: Replacing a light switch with a dimmer
subtitle: Swap a one-gang on/off light switch for a trailing-edge dimmer on an existing lighting circuit
excerpt: How to replace a standard one-gang light switch with a trailing-edge LED dimmer on an existing lighting circuit, covering safe isolation, identifying the switch wiring, connecting the dimmer terminals, and setting the dimmer range for the load.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. BS 7671 (IET Wiring Regulations, 18th Edition). IET On-Site Guide.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dimmer that cuts the trailing edge of the AC waveform to reduce power to the load. Suitable for LED and capacitive loads; generates less noise and heat than leading-edge (TRIAC) dimmers. LED lamps must be marked as dimmable on the packaging: non-dimmable LEDs flicker or fail when dimmed.

In UK lighting wiring, the switch wire is the conductor that carries the switched live between the ceiling rose and the switch. In older wiring, both conductors in the switch drop may be black or red; the switch live is conventionally marked with red or brown sleeving at the switch end. In modern cables, the switch wire is a brown-sleeved blue core.

content.text: Replacing a one-gang switch with a

is Part P exempt work in England (like-for-like replacement on an existing circuit). The critical differences from a standard switch are the extra earth terminal on the dimmer body and the minimum-load setting for LED lamps. The
connects to L1 and the supply live connects to L on the dimmer.

A trailing-edge LED dimmer rated for the total wattage of the load (check the combined wattage of all the lamps on the circuit). Dimmers have a minimum load (often 10 W or 25 W): running fewer lamps than the minimum causes flickering. Check that all lamps are marked as dimmable. The dimmer must fit the existing single-gang back box: most dimmers occupy a deeper back box (35 mm minimum) than a standard switch.

Test the two-pole voltage tester against the proving unit to confirm it is working. At the consumer unit, switch off the MCB for the lighting circuit and fit the lock-off device. At the switch position, test between each conductor with the tester: all readings must be zero. Test the tester against the proving unit again to confirm it is still working. Only then is the switch safe to open.

Undo the two retaining screws and pull the switch faceplate away from the back box. Identify the terminals: a one-gang switch has a common terminal (COM or L) and a switched terminal (SW or L1). In older wiring, two black or red conductors enter the switch; in modern wiring, a brown and a blue-with-brown-sleeve. Photograph the terminal connections before releasing any core. Release both cores, note the earth conductor condition.

The dimmer has three terminals: L (supply live, incoming from ceiling), L1 (switched live, going to ceiling), and earth. Connect the supply live (the one that was on COM) to the dimmer's L terminal. Connect the switch wire (the one that was on SW) to the dimmer's L1 terminal. Connect the earth conductor to the earth terminal on the dimmer body. Green-and-yellow sleeving must be fitted on any bare earth. Fold the cables into the back box and fit the dimmer faceplate with its retaining screws.
Step 4, set the dimmer range and test

Most LED dimmers have a small adjustment trimmer on the back of the faceplate, accessible through a small hole. Remove the lock-off device and switch the MCB back on. Switch on the dimmer and adjust the trimmer for minimum brightness: the lamps should dim down without flickering and without going out before the dimmer knob reaches its lowest position. If the lamps flicker, adjust the trimmer by a quarter-turn and test again.

A two-gang dimmer replaces a two-gang switch to control two separate circuits from one faceplate. Each gang has its own set of L and L1 terminals. Some dimmers have a wireless receiver module built in: the wiring is the same but the dimmer can also be controlled from a remote or smart-home app.

If lamps flicker after fitting a dimmer, check that every lamp on the circuit is marked as dimmable. A single non-dimmable lamp in a fitting causes the whole circuit to flicker. If the dimmer runs warm to the touch, the total load is close to its rated maximum: check the wattage sum of all lamps and ensure they are below the dimmer's rating.

attrs.symptom: Lamps flicker at low settings after fitting the dimmer
attrs.cause: The minimum load trimmer is set too low; or one or more lamps are not rated as dimmable.
attrs.fix: Adjust the minimum load trimmer upward a quarter-turn. If the flickering is intermittent, one lamp is non-dimmable: replace all lamps with dimmable LED versions.

attrs.symptom: MCB trips when the dimmer is switched on
attrs.cause: A short circuit at the new dimmer's terminal connections; or the wrong terminal has been used.
attrs.fix: Switch off the MCB and fit the lock-off device. Open the dimmer and check that no bare conductor is touching the back box. Confirm that the earth wire is on the earth terminal and not on L or L1. Correct and reinstate.
